items.q: What is the difference between energy and power?
items.correct: Energy is measured in joules; power is the rate of energy transfer, measured in watts (joules per second).
items.misconception: Energy and power are the same — both describe how much fuel something uses.
items.random_wrong: Power is the energy used over the lifetime of a device.
items.why: Students use 'power' in everyday language to mean 'a lot of energy', missing that power has the dimension of energy per time.

items.q: According to Faraday's law, what determines the size of the induced emf in a coil?
items.correct: The rate of change of magnetic flux linkage through the coil.
items.misconception: The strength of the magnetic field alone.
items.random_wrong: The number of turns in the coil alone.
items.why: Students focus on the field strength or the coil's properties in isolation and miss that it is the rate of change of flux linkage that matters.

items.q: What is the currently accepted age of the universe?
items.misconception: About 14 billion years — close enough for any purpose.
items.random_wrong: About 6 billion years, the same as the Earth.
items.why: Students round aggressively and don't appreciate that the precision (13.8, not 14) reflects measurements from the cosmic microwave background.
Correct answer: Sun. “Moon” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: Straight lines. “Curves” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Light hits a mirror at 30 degrees to the normal. What is the angle of reflection in degrees?
items.why: Exact answer: 30. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
